speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture is a picture of my family. It was taken in summer of 1938 in Krakow, Poland. On the left, sitting on the left, is my father, standing behind him is my sister. I am next to it, and to the right is my mother. It was taken on the roof garden of the family, [? Russell ?] [? Berbin, ?] whose house we were, and what you see in the background is the Wawel Castle. It was the castle of the old Polish kings, and-- and later occupied by the German general governor of Poland. This picture is of me, of course, in the uniform. I do not know when it was taken. It must have been taken soon after the end of the-- soon after the end of the war. I weighed about 85 pounds at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it was taken? Tell us where it was taken. What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was either-- either in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- I don't know. It probably is-- I really can't tell you. I don't know. The latest it was taken would have been when we came to Italy, but I think I have an idea it was taken a little before, somewhere between 1945 and in the late '45s, I think it was taken. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This picture was taken in, it must have been in fall 1945. It was the first picture taken after our-- after our liberation in Italy. It's on a street corner somewhere in Rome. I don't know where. I'm on the left, my sister Susie is next to me, and then it's my mother, and to the right, my father. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS] This picture was taken on February 6, 1947. It was the day of our departure from Italy to the United States. The picture is taken in front of the Port of Naples, Italy, just before we boarded. You can see in the background the outlines of a ship. I don't know whether it was the boat we were on, but this is taken just before. The last year I was in Italy. [PAUSES FOR 33 SECONDS] This is a pass issued by the temporary administration of the former-- former prison for-- for-- for political [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prisoners in Brunnlitz, and it's dated May 25, 1945. And it said there Heinz Drezsler, which was my name, born so-and-so in Dresden, was discharged from the concentration camp in Brunnlitz and wants to return to his place of home. All military and civilian organizations are asked not to put any obstacles in the way of-- of his return, and to make the-- and to make these things easier for him. It's signed by somebody, the temporary administration of the former concentration camp for political prisoners in Brunnlitz. It was very important in Europe during that time to have all kinds of IDs. The more IDs you had in your pocket, the more important it was, the better you were off. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] Again, the importance of identification cards in the post-war era of Europe. This personal identification card was issued in June 27, 1945, in Linz, identifying me as a former internee of the camp, Mauthausen. I was not in Mauthausen, but the Linz area was under the supervision, under the jurisdiction of Mauthausen. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On this identification card is my fingerprint. And it has a rubber stamp on the left side that says the Office of Polish Jews, Commissioner [POLISH] or something like this. My Polish is not that great. And this is what-- what my identification, it was. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This is a collection of signatures and my father did this. It says on top, 'To remember the time spent together in the concentration camps of Krakow-Plaszow, Gross Rosen, Auschwitz, and Brunnlitz, March-- March 13, 1941 to May 10, 1945. And there are several signatures. I remember the top one, Dr. Lewkowiecz The first signature on this sheet is by Dr. Ferdynand Lewkowiecz, who was a doctor in Krakow, and I guess in Plaszow, too. And he later came to-- to America, and we have seen him in-- in the United States. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This signature, the signature by-- of Herman Rosner, his wife, and son, I think it says. He was one of the two-- the musician, that he played the violin. He-- he played the violin in the-- in the picture, too. And he is still in New York now. He was a very good musician, together with his brother. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Toward the end of April, I think it was around April 21 or 22, I started a diary in Brunnlitz. It was written in German shorthand, in pencil. And this is two pages of this diary. I continued it for then a few months after the war, and I marked down a lot of the things that-- that happened during the end of the war, in the last few months-- weeks and months, during and after the war. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] These three items were made in-- in Brunnlitz. They were handmade. I do not know by whom. It was a metalworker who did them for my father or me. The top one is a mirror framed with metal, and I don't know whether this is glass or what he-- where he got this from. The middle one is a comb. As you see, it's cut out of metal. And the bottom one is a knife, and the handle, it seems to be wrapped around with-- with-- with wire, with metal wire. It shows that as good as Brunnlitz was, it was still primitive if we had to have tools or utensils like this. It's a very unusual thing. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] These four cards are part of a set of playing cards that were hand-made by one of our prisoners who was very good in making rubber stamps. And he cut these out, a complete set of playing cards. I guess we used them. I don't remember. But it shows that the spirit for-- of survival was still there, that we still had time to-- to play, a little bit at least. [PAUSES FOR 26 SECONDS] This picture is of Thomas Keneally, the author of the book, together with Martha and me. We met him twice in Florida, once in Boca Raton, and then again in West Palm Beach, where we were on the stage with him both times. And the inscription is 'To the dear Dresslers from Thomas Keneally, 1994.' [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The number, 69046, that you see on this uniform was my number. Next to it you see, on this little thing there, you see a yellow triangle that indicates it was a Jewish prisoner. Each category of prisoners had their own colors, red, blue, pink, and-- and different-- different colors. It was a uniform that we wore in Brunnlitz and after our liberation in-- in Czechoslovakia, until we wore-- until we were able to get some civilian clothes. I'm sure it was made in the concentration camp, manufactured in one concentration camp. I had it packed up for about 30 years, and I never touched it. And I was afraid to open it. I thought it would be fall apart, but it was still intact. It proves that if it was made in the concentration camp, it was good workmanship. The cap was a cap that we wore to keep us sort of warm. This was my uniform. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Henry. This has been an exceptional experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And I think you did a marvelous job, all of you, in bringing these things out of me. And I can't wait to see my copy of the tape.
